text: Large Language Models (LLMs) generate responses based on input context. In a RAG system, the retrieved documents are provided as context to the LLM. The model uses this information to generate accurate and relevant answers. Without proper context, LLMs may hallucinate or produce incorrect outputs. Therefore, high-quality retrieval is critical for effective RAG systems.

Metadata in a retrieval system provides structured information about documents such as source, author, date, and category. It allows filtering before performing vector search, improving efficiency and relevance. For example, a system can filter documents by topic or time range before running semantic search, reducing the search space and improving performance.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a method that combines information retrieval with text generation. Instead of relying only on a pre-trained language model, RAG retrieves relevant documents from an external knowledge base and uses them as context for generating responses. This approach improves accuracy, reduces hallucination, and ensures responses are grounded in real data.

Vector search is a technique used to find similar items based on their embeddings. Text data is converted into numerical vectors using embedding models. These vectors are stored in a vector database such as FAISS. When a query is given, it is also converted into a vector and compared against stored vectors using similarity metrics like cosine similarity or inner product to retrieve the most relevant results.
